stem: The function \( h(x) = \sqrt{[[a]]x + ([[b]])} \) is written as a composition \( h(x) = f(g(x)) \) whose outer function is \( f(u) = \sqrt{u} \). What is the value of the inner function \( g \) at \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: The outer function is \( f(u)=\sqrt{u} \), so the inner function is everything inside the root: \( g(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \). Therefore \( g([[x0]]) = ([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the constant term of \( g \).
This drops the coefficient \( [[a]] \) on \( x \).
This keeps only the \( x \)-term and forgets the constant \( ([[b]]) \).
This adds the coefficient in as if it were a separate term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( h(x) = \left([[a]]x + ([[b]])\right)^{[[n]]} \) is written as a composition \( h(x) = f(g(x)) \) with inner function \( g(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \) and outer function \( f(u) = u^{[[n]]} \). What is \( f([[u0]]) \)?
solution: The outer function is \( f(u) = u^{[[n]]} \), so evaluating it at \( u = [[u0]] \) means raising [[u0]] to the [[n]]-th power directly (the coefficients \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \) belong to the inner function and play no role here): \( f([[u0]]) = ([[u0]])^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by the exponent instead of raising to that power.
This uses one less than the correct exponent.
This uses one more than the correct exponent.
This negates the correct value even though the exponent does not force a sign change.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( k(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \), \( g(u) = u^{2} + ([[c]]) \), and \( f(v) = [[d]]v + ([[e]]) \), so that \( h(x) = f(g(k(x))) \) is a triple composition. What is the value of the middle function applied to the inner one, i.e. \( g(k([[x0]])) \)? (Do not apply \( f \).)
solution: First apply the innermost function: \( k([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]]) + ([[b]]) \). Then apply the middle function to that result: \( g(k([[x0]])) = \big(k([[x0]])\big)^{2} + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \). The outer function \( f \) is not applied.
distractor_rationales: This goes one step too far and also applies the outer function \( f \).
This applies the middle function's squaring correctly but forgets to add the constant \( ([[c]]) \).
This stops after the innermost function and never applies the middle one.
This squares only the \( x \)-term of \( k \), forgetting the constant \( ([[b]]) \) before squaring.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( h(x) = \dfrac{1}{[[a]]x + ([[b]])} \) is written as \( h(x) = f(g(x)) \) with outer function \( f(u) = \dfrac{1}{u} \) and inner function \( g(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \). For what value of \( x \) does the inner function satisfy \( g(x) = [[target]] \)?
solution: Set the inner function equal to the target: \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) = [[target]] \), so \( x = \dfrac{[[target]] - ([[b]])}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds \( [[b]] \) instead of subtracting it before dividing by \( [[a]] \).
This subtracts \( [[b]] \) from the target but forgets to divide by \( [[a]] \).
This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( x \)).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The function \( h(x) = |[[a]]x + ([[b]])| \) is written as \( h(x) = f(g(x)) \) with inner function \( g(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \) and outer function \( f(u) = |u| \). What is \( f([[u0]]) \)?
solution: The outer function is \( f(u) = |u| \), which strips the sign of its input regardless of the inner function's coefficients. So \( f([[u0]]) = |[[u0]]| = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the absolute value and reports the raw (possibly negative) input.
This negates the input outright instead of taking its absolute value, which is wrong whenever the input is already positive.
This confuses the outer function's input with the inner function's coefficient \( [[a]] \).
This squares the input instead of taking its absolute value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
